subject: Acute Abdomen
title: Peritonitis: CXR vs Lipase Decoded
hook: One erect chest X-ray can point your peritonitic patient toward theatre. Here is the exam logic.

points.heading: Spot peritonitis from the door
points.icon: a patient lying still on a bed
bullets: Continuous pain, worse on movement or coughing.
Colic is the opposite: doubled up, restless.

points.heading: Order the right two tests
points.icon: a test tube and an X-ray film
bullets: Generalised peritonitis: erect chest X-ray plus serum lipase.
No lipase? Amylase is your fallback.
Together they separate surgical from non-surgical causes.

points.heading: Air under the diaphragm
points.icon: a diaphragm with free gas beneath it
bullets: Free gas usually means a perforated viscus, urgent surgery.
A normal film does not exclude perforation.
Raised lipase with no free gas? Think pancreatitis.

points.heading: Pancreatitis: hands off the knife
bullets: Pancreatitis can cause generalised peritonitis too.
But it usually needs no laparotomy.
Spotting it avoids a needless operation. Classic exam point.

captionLines: One erect chest X-ray can point your peritonitic patient toward theatre. 🩻
Generalised peritonitis is a guaranteed exam topic, and the erect chest X-ray versus serum lipase split is how examiners test your reasoning.
Free air usually means perforation and urgent surgery, though a normal film does not exclude it; a high lipase points to pancreatitis you usually do not operate on.
